channel.title: Latest Updates
channel.description: The most recent blogs and online articles from The Economist

item.title: The Chinese EV company betting big on robots
item.description: Our weekly podcast on China. This week, we sit down with the businessman known as Chinaâs answer to Elon MuskÂ

item.title: Blighty newsletter: Six things to watch in Thursdayâs elections
item.description: Owen Winter, our political correspondent, on what you need to know about May 7th

item.title: To combat antisemitism, first grasp where it comes from
item.description: What looks like a 21st-century problem has deep, dark roots

item.title: The architects of the Vietnam War knew it was doomed
item.description: Kennedy, Johnson and McNamara were private realists but chose the path of least resistance, writes Fredrik Logevall

item.title: In an age of status symbols, tiaras take the crown
item.description: Women worldwide are channelling royal glamour

item.title: Mea culpa
item.description: Our report from 1974

item.title: Asiaâs stranded seafarers suffer as the Iran war drags on
item.description: In a more dangerous world, unsung mariners are under increasing threat

item.description: Our daily political update, featuring the stories that matter

item.description: Also on the daily podcast: how wars made America and oldies (mis)use emojisÂ

item.description: Also on the daily podcast: the influencer speaking truth to Vladimir Putin and the real costs of Indiaâs noise pollution

item.description: Argentinaâs president claims he is the true victim of a struggling economy

item.description: Shashank Joshi, our defence editor, invites a Ukrainian drone expert to write a guest editionÂ

item.description: Eurocrats are belatedly developing a healthy distaste for red tape

item.title: Narendra Modi has extended his grip on India
item.description: Indiaâs ruling party scores a historic victory in West Bengalâbut should beware votersâ unhappiness with incumbentsÂ

item.title: Bad government statistics can cost the economy billions
item.description: A new study tries to put a number on the value of reliable numbers

item.title: Can Donald Trump reopen the Strait of Hormuz?
item.description: Probably not, and he still has to deal with Iranâs uranium

item.title: China thinks America is declining but still uniquely dangerous
item.description: It sees Donald Trump as both symptom and accelerant of the decline

item.title: To improve Britainâs politics, improve its voting system
item.description: Ranking candidates by order of preference would better reflect the will of the people, writes Eric Maskin

item.title: Turn on, tune in, trust no one: the paranoid style captures TV
item.description: Conspiracy thrillers are the favourite genre of a distrustful age

item.description: A handpicked article read aloud from the latest issue of The Economist

item.description: The chancellorâs reckless truth-telling means trouble for Germany

item.title: What to do about Britainâs rising antisemitism?
item.description: A stabbing attack in a Jewish neighbourhood of London lends fresh urgency to the question

item.title: Global carmakers desperately want to be more Chinese
item.description: But partnering with local companies carries big risks

item.title: The remarkable revival of eBay
item.description: The internetâs flea market is backâand GameStop wants to buy it

item.title: Young men are souring on Donald Trump
item.description: But can Democrats win them over?

item.title: How to save the safari
item.description: Balancing the needs of locals, tourists and animals is a difficult business

item.title: The case against trees
item.description: Indiaâs bureaucrats are bad at trade-offs between nature and roads

item.title: Germany claims it has the worldâs best bread
item.description: May 5th is national Bread Day, and Germans are ready to roll

item.description: Charlotte Howard, our New York bureau chief, on Americaâs legal system

item.title: Plot Twist newsletter: The real value of a baseball-card collection
item.description: Jon Fasman, our senior culture correspondent, on what makes someone pay millions of dollars for a collectible

item.title: 17 siblings and counting
item.description: How donor conception could expand the meaning of family

item.title: Does acupuncture work?
item.description: It seems useful for pain. The juryâs out on everything else

item.title: Labour faces a drubbing in Englandâs local elections
item.description: The party could lose more than half of the council seats it is defending

item.title: Craig Venter raced to decode the human genome
item.description: The dark horse of the Human Genome Project died on April 29th, aged 79

item.description: Our weekly podcast on America. This week, how are rural voters faring under Donald Trump?

item.description: Our predictive index has reached a sizzling 3.6%

item.description: Our podcast on markets, the economy and business. This week, how the City bounced back from Brexit

item.description: Some say the whopping project should be scaled down

item.description: Lulaâs voters and Bolsonaroâs voters donât agree on much. But they all hate the corruption that plagues Brazil
